error.signature: AI uses 'Constantinople' for Istanbul, 'Smyrna' for İzmir, 'Adrianople' for Edirne, or other Ottoman/Byzantine-era city names in modern Turkish business or media

verdict.summary: Turkey officially adopted modern Turkish city names in 1930 (1923 republic transition). 'Constantinople' (Byzantine/Greek name) is now read as nostalgia for the pre-Turkish era, often associated with Greek/Armenian historical claims. Same applies to: Smyrna (Greek, now İzmir), Adrianople (now Edirne), Trebizond (now Trabzon), Antioch (now Antakya, in Syrian-border Hatay province), Nicaea (now İznik). Using old names in modern correspondence is read as ignorant or politically loaded.

dead_ends.action: Address business correspondence to 'Constantinople, Turkey' or use 'Constantinople' in marketing copy targeting Turkish audiences
dead_ends.why_fails: Turkey requested international postal recognition of 'Istanbul' since 1930. Continued use of 'Constantinople' is politically loaded — associated with Greek and Armenian diaspora historical claims. Modern Turkish business correspondence uses İstanbul (with the dotted İ).

dead_ends.action: Refer to ancient Hagia Sophia / Constantinople interchangeably with modern Istanbul in tourist content distributed in Turkey
dead_ends.why_fails: Hagia Sophia (Aya Sofya) was reconverted to a mosque in 2020 — its historical Greek Orthodox / later Roman Catholic role is acknowledged but Turkish framing emphasizes Ottoman period and current Islamic role. Tourist content emphasizing only the Christian past is politically incomplete and increasingly noticed.

dead_ends.action: Use Greek city names (Smyrna, Adrianople) in genealogy services or maps for diaspora Greek/Armenian Turks
dead_ends.why_fails: While historically accurate, using 'Smyrna' for modern İzmir in materials distributed in Turkey can be read as denying Turkish identity. The 1922 Smyrna fire / population exchange is one of the foundational and tense events in Greek-Turkish relations.

workarounds.action: Use modern Turkish names for cities in any modern context: İstanbul, İzmir, Edirne, Trabzon, Antakya, İznik, Bursa, Şanlıurfa, Diyarbakır, Adana. The dotted İ vs undotted I matters in Turkish — 'Istanbul' (no dot, ASCII) is widely accepted internationally.

For historical / academic work explicitly about pre-1453 Constantinople, the Byzantine name is acceptable — context makes the temporal scope clear. 'Byzantine Constantinople, modern İstanbul' is a common academic format.

Hagia Sophia: respect the current status as a mosque (since July 2020). Visitors enter shoeless, dressed modestly, head-covered for women. Christian mosaics are visible but partially covered during prayer.
